title: Numbers, Time, and Age
learningGoal: The learner will master Japanese numbers from 1-99, learn how to state their age, tell time (hours), and name the days of the week.
instructions: Read through each section carefully. Numbers in Japanese are very logical, but there are a few tricky exceptions when telling time or stating your age. Make sure to review the examples and listen to the audio carefully.

lessonContent.bodyText: Learning Japanese numbers 1-10 is the foundation for all counting. Note that 4, 7, and 9 have two different readings. 'Yon', 'Nana', and 'Kyuu' are the most common.

targetLanguageExamples.targetText: なな(しち)、はち、きゅう(く)、じゅう

lessonContent.bodyText: After 10, Japanese numbers stack like building blocks. 11 is '10 + 1' (juu-ichi). 20 is '2 + 10' (ni-juu). 21 is '2 + 10 + 1' (ni-juu-ichi). It's very mathematical!

To state your age, add the word 'sai' (さい) to the number. For example, 5 years old is 'go-sai'. However, there are exceptions! 1, 8, and 10 change their sounds. Also, 20 years old is a special word: 'hatachi'.

targetLanguageExamples.targetText: わたしは じゅうごさいです。
わたしは はたちです。
いっさい、はっさい、じゅっさい
targetLanguageExamples.baseTranslation: 1 year old, 8 years old, 10 years old (Exceptions)

lessonContent.bodyText: To express the hour, simply add 'ji' (じ) after the number. It means 'o'clock'. Watch out for 4, 7, and 9! They use the alternate readings: yo-ji, shichi-ji, and ku-ji.
Every day of the week ends in 'youbi' (ようび), meaning 'day'. The first part is an element of nature. Moon, Fire, Water, Wood, Gold, Earth, Sun.

targetLanguageExamples.targetText: すいようび、もくようび
きんようび、どようび、にちようび
targetLanguageExamples.baseTranslation: Friday (Gold day), Saturday (Earth day), Sunday (Sun day)

learningObjective: The learner will accurately count to 99, tell the time on the hour, state their age including exceptions like 'hatachi', and list the days of the week.

examples.targetText: いまは げつようびです。はちじです。わたしは はたちです。
examples.transliteration: Ima wa getsuyoubi desu. Hachi-ji desu. Watashi wa hatachi desu.
examples.baseTranslation: Now it is Monday. It is 8 o'clock. I am 20 years old.
examples.explanation: This ties together Time ('ima' = now), Day of the week, Hour ('ji'), and Age ('hatachi').

importantRules.ruleText: For 4 o'clock, NEVER say 'yon-ji'. It is ALWAYS 'yo-ji'.
importantRules.exceptionNotes: The same applies to 9 o'clock: It is 'ku-ji', not 'kyuu-ji'.

importantRules.ruleText: 20 years old is a special word: 'hatachi' (はたち). Do not say 'ni-juu-sai'.
importantRules.exceptionNotes: If you are 21, it goes back to normal: 'ni-juu-issai'.

cultureContext.contextText: In Japan, turning 20 is a huge cultural milestone. It is the age of adulthood. Every January, there is a national holiday called 'Coming of Age Day' (Seijin no Hi) where all the 20-year-olds ('hatachi') dress up in beautiful kimonos and suits to attend a city ceremony.
dosList: Memorize 'hatachi' because 20 is the most culturally significant age in Japan.
dontsList: Avoid giving gifts in sets of 4 or 9. The number 4 (shi) sounds like the word for 'death', and 9 (ku) sounds like the word for 'suffering'.

summary: Japanese numbers stack logically (e.g., 20 is 2-10). To state your age, add 'sai', but remember that 20 is 'hatachi'. To state the time, add 'ji', but remember that 4:00 is 'yo-ji' and 9:00 is 'ku-ji'. The days of the week end in 'youbi'.

checkpointQuestions.questionText: How do you say 45 in Japanese?
How do you say 'I am 20 years old'?
checkpointQuestions.expectedAnswer: わたしは はたちです (Watashi wa hatachi desu)

keyTakeaways: Days of the week ending in 'youbi'
adminCorrectAnswerSet.conceptSummary: Mastering numbers, time, age, and days of the week with an emphasis on phonetic exceptions.
importantFacts: 4 is yon/shi, 7 is nana/shichi, 9 is kyuu/ku.

checkpointAnswers.q2: わたしは はたちです

adminCorrectAnswerSet.expectedUnderstanding: Learner must memorize 'hatachi', 'yoji', 'kuji', and the 'youbi' suffixes.

feedbackRules.onPass: Great work! You have unlocked numbers, time, and days.
feedbackRules.onFail: Review the exceptions. Pay special attention to 4 o'clock and 20 years old.
feedbackRules.onPartial: Good. Remember that numbers like 4 and 9 change depending on what you are counting.

audioAssets.audioTranscript: いち。に。さん。よん。ご。ろく。なな。はち。きゅう。じゅう。にじゅう。さんじゅう。じゅうごさい。はたち。いちじ。よじ。くじ。げつようび。かようび。すいようび。
